title: Non-Hermitian Crystalline Braid Topology from Hermitian Projection: A Zero-Mode Resonance Mechanism
abstract: Non-Hermitian topological phases are usually engineered through gain, loss, asymmetric couplings, or explicit environmental channels. Here we show that non-Hermitian crystalline braid topology can instead emerge from projection alone, starting from a fully Hermitian and topologically trivial parent lattice. The mechanism is zero-mode-resonant projection. When the eliminated complement is zero-mode free, projection has a smooth low-frequency limit and reduces to a static Schur complement, yielding conventional SSH-type descendants. When a complement zero mode couples to the retained subsystem, the embedding self-energy develops a pole, the zero-frequency limit becomes singular, and topology is carried by the finite-frequency projected Green's function-where frequency is a tunable parameter, the drive frequency in a circuit realization, for instance. We demonstrate this mechanism in an exactly solvable model, a trivial nearest-neighbor square lattice with an embedded one-dimensional zig-zag brane. Odd-parity periodic sectors are resonant: a sublattice-imbalance zero mode generates the singular self-energy, and the complex spectrum forms an abelian two-band braid whose transitions occur only at isolated finite frequencies. Although the internal class is $\text{AI}^†$ featuring only trivial phases, embedding parity induces conjugated pseudo-Hermiticity (CPH), quantizes the complex Berry phase, and identifies it with the braid count. The model is free of the non-Hermitian skin effect, making the invariant a genuine Bloch bulk quantity. In topolectrical realizations, the same finite-frequency braid transitions appear as transmission zeros and admittance features at the predicted drive frequencies.
summary: The paper introduces a mechanism where non-Hermitian crystalline braid topology appears by projecting degrees of freedom from a simple Hermitian lattice. This process becomes singular (resonant) when eliminated zero modes couple to the remaining system, forcing the topological invariant into the finite-frequency response function. This offers a novel route to engineer complex quantum phases in physical realizations like circuits.
main_problem: To demonstrate that non-Hermitian crystalline braid topology can emerge solely from projecting a fully Hermitian, topologically trivial parent lattice without introducing explicit gain or loss.
main_result: The non-Hermitian topology is carried by the finite-frequency projected Green's function, arising specifically through a zero-mode resonance mechanism during projection. This results in a robust crystalline braid invariant that is immune to the non-Hermitian skin effect.
method: The analysis employs the Schur complement technique to project degrees of freedom from a parent Hamiltonian onto a lower-dimensional brane subsystem. The key diagnostic tool is examining the singularity structure of the embedding self-energy at finite frequencies ($\omega$).
model_or_system: The model uses a nearest-neighbor square lattice containing an embedded one-dimensional zig-zag brane, analyzed in momentum space. The effective non-Hermiticity arises from frequency-dependent coupling terms derived via projection.
key_observables: Finite-frequency projected Green's function, complex Berry phase (quantized to a braid count), transmission zeros, and admittance features at specific drive frequencies ($\omega$).
important_parameters: The resonance condition depends on the parity of the eliminated complement ($N$ odd vs. even) and the tunable frequency parameter $\omega$.
main_assumptions: The analysis distinguishes between regular projection (static limit) and resonant projection, which requires a zero mode in the eliminated complement to couple to the retained subsystem.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate energy band evolution, density of states comparisons for even vs. odd parity sectors, and complex spectra comparing analytical bulk solutions to numerical eigenvalues for both open and periodic boundary conditions.
paper_structure: The paper systematically contrasts two projection regimes: the regular Schur complement route (recovering SSH-like physics) versus the resonant zero-mode projection. The core argument focuses on how the resonance forces topology into the finite-frequency response, culminating in the derivation of a crystalline braid invariant protected by symmetry.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful theoretical framework showing that complex topological features can emerge purely from mathematical constraints (projection) rather than requiring explicit physical non-Hermiticity terms like gain/loss, which is highly relevant for designing novel quantum metamaterials and circuit elements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Two-dimensional parent lattice with an embed- ded one-dimensional “zig-zag” brane (red); the remaining sites form the complement, and the green rectangle marks the brane unit cell. Purple arrows show the translation vector e [Eq. (17)] and the hopping parameters tx, ty. The numbers N1 and N2 count the complement chains to the left and right of the brane.

Figure 2: Phase diagram of the PT + symmetry for the open-boundary projected Hamiltonian. The value plot- ted on the y-axis is given by ωeff = ω 2ty sinh ϕcr . If both N1 and N2 are even, only the PT +-unbroken phase is realized. When at least one of them is odd, PT +-broken regions appear. The critical lines are determined by the condition |FN1 −FN2| = 2.
Figure 4: Energy bands and lifetimes for periodic bound- ary conditions with odd N, obtained from Eqs. (43) and (44). As ω is reduced, gaps close sequentially when | ˜GN| = 1 and α = (2ℓ+ 1)π/2.

Figure 3: Energy bands and lifetimes for periodic bound- ary conditions with even N, obtained from Eqs. (38) and (39). The bands retain a regular SSH-like structure; in the zero-frequency descendant, ordinary gap closings are controlled by zeros of the effective hopping Q(k).

Figure 5: Two-band braiding of the periodic odd-N spectrum. The horizontal plane is the complex-energy plane and the vertical direction is the crystal momentum. The three panels follow the red trajectory in Fig. 7: the complex bands are unbraided before the first transition, acquire a nontrivial braid invariant after the first finite-frequency strand touching, and return to a trivial braid invariant after the next transition. In the first panel each band coincides with a separate strand and the loops do not interlink.
